evals.prompt: I'm starting a new Python project. Can you set up linting and formatting with modern tooling in my pyproject.toml?
evals.expected_output: Configures ruff for linting and formatting with sensible defaults, and sets up mypy or pyright for type checking
expectations: Configures ruff as the primary linter and formatter
Sets line-length to 120
Selects appropriate ruff lint rules (E, W, F, I, B, C4, UP, SIM or similar)
Configures ruff format settings (quote-style, indent-style)
Sets up mypy or pyright with strict mode
Uses pyproject.toml as the configuration file
Targets a modern Python version (3.10+)

evals.prompt:
Review this code for style issues:

def getUserByEmail(e):
  from myproject.models import User
  from ..utils import validate
  import os
  x = validate(e)
  if x:
    u = User.objects.filter(email=e).first()
    return u
  return None
evals.expected_output: Identifies naming convention violations, import organization issues, relative imports, poor variable names, and provides corrected code
expectations: Flags getUserByEmail as violating snake_case convention
Flags single-letter variable names (e, x, u) as unclear
Points out import organization issues (standard lib, third-party, local not grouped)
Recommends absolute imports over relative imports
Provides a corrected version of the code
Suggests more descriptive variable names

evals.prompt:
Write a Google-style docstring for this function:

def retry_request(url, max_retries=3, backoff_factor=1.5, timeout=30):
    # retries an HTTP GET request with exponential backoff
    ...
evals.expected_output: Writes a complete Google-style docstring with Args, Returns, Raises, and optionally an Example section
expectations: Uses Google-style docstring format
Includes an Args section documenting all parameters
Includes a Returns section
Includes a Raises section for expected exceptions
Describes what the function does in the summary line
Documents default values for optional parameters

evals.expected_output: Adds proper type annotations using modern Python syntax, annotating parameters, return types, and handling Optional correctly
expectations: Uses modern union syntax (User | None instead of Optional[User])
Annotates all function parameters
Annotates all return types
Uses list[Item] and list[Result] style generics (not List[Item])
Annotates max_workers as int
Annotates items with a typed list

evals.prompt: Create a generic Repository base class in Python that can work with any entity type and ID type, then show a concrete UserRepository implementation.
evals.expected_output: Implements a Generic[T, ID] abstract base class with typed methods, and a concrete subclass that binds the type parameters
expectations: Uses TypeVar for T and ID
Inherits from Generic[T, ID] and ABC
Decorates abstract methods with @abstractmethod
Concrete class specifies Repository[User, str]
All methods have proper type annotations
get method returns T | None

evals.prompt: Define a Serializable protocol in Python that any class can satisfy without inheriting from it, and demonstrate usage with a User class.
evals.expected_output: Defines a Protocol class with to_dict and from_dict methods, and shows a User class that satisfies it structurally
expectations: Uses typing.Protocol
Defines to_dict and from_dict methods on the protocol
User class does not inherit from Serializable
Function accepting Serializable works with User
Optionally adds @runtime_checkable for isinstance support
